alert.title: [real-k8s] error rate 22% + p99 latency spike on internal Kinesis cell — kinesis-dataplane

commonAnnotations.summary: kinesis-dataplane PutRecord/GetRecords error_rate 22% (SLO 0.5%) and p99 latency 9,400ms on internal cell kinesis-cell-7; downstream CloudWatch Logs, Firehose, Lambda, ECS, Redshift, Glue degraded
commonAnnotations.description: An internally-used Kinesis Data Streams cell (kinesis-cell-7) is throwing elevated PutRecord/GetRecords errors and high latency, and downstream internal consumers (CloudWatch Logs, Firehose, Lambda, ECS, Redshift, Glue) are degraded. A routine single-AZ deployment rolled through the dataplane statefulset earlier today, which makes this look like a bad rollout. The loud symptom is on the dataplane pods and on the TLS connection-provisioner, which is pegged at its connection ceiling and looks like the capacity bottleneck. Investigate to find the true root cause.
commonAnnotations.error: KinesisCellHighErrorRate: PutRecord/GetRecords error_rate 22% on internal cell kinesis-cell-7

adversarial_signals: A routine single-AZ deployment rolled through the kinesis-dataplane statefulset starting 09:09 PDT, taking hosts in and out of service; because customer-visible impact began hours later it looks like a bad_deploy and tempts an immediate rollback — but the deployed image is byte-identical to the prior good revision and rolling it back does not stop the reshuffle storm. The deploy was only the TRIGGER that removed hosts; the defect is in how the cell manager rebalanced afterward.

The TLS connection-provisioner (conn-provisioner) is pegged at 100% of its connection ceiling and is the component throwing the most visible PutRecord/GetRecords errors, so it looks like the root cause / a capacity shortfall to be fixed by adding raw provisioner capacity alone; in reality it is a DOWNSTREAM victim being hammered by the shard-reshuffle storm, and adding capacity without first shedding load to shrink the status messages only buys time while the storm keeps churning.

Several dataplane hosts (kds-host-3/4) are reported UNHEALTHY by the cell manager and look like genuinely failed nodes that should be restarted or drained; in truth those hosts are healthy and serving — they were FALSELY marked unhealthy because their oversized status messages could not be ingested by the cell manager in time, and restarting/draining them adds even more rebalance churn.

Shard rebalance / redistribution rate is spiking, which looks like the cell manager correctly recovering from failed hosts; it is actually the storm itself — a self-amplifying loop where false-unhealthy verdicts trigger redistribution that creates more oversized status messages that cause more false-unhealthy verdicts.

Downstream services CloudWatch Logs, Firehose, S3 event delivery, ECS (awslogs blocking mode), Lambda, Redshift and Glue are all erroring, which looks like a broad multi-service control-plane outage; they are all simply consumers of this one impaired internal Kinesis cell and recover once the cell recovers.

All Kubernetes nodes report Ready with normal CPU and memory and no other Kinesis cell is affected (cell isolation held at the infra layer); there is no node_failure or cluster resource_exhaustion — the saturation is in the cell-management control loop and the connection-provisioner it overran.

pods.name: kinesis-cellmanager-0
pods.note: SMOKING-GUN host. The cell manager process is up, but its status-ingestion queue is backed up: it is dropping/delaying oversized status messages from the few hosts now carrying most shards, then declaring those healthy hosts unhealthy and issuing redistribution commands. The fault is its balance-by-throughput-not-shard-count logic, not a crash.

pods.name: kinesis-dataplane-kds-host-3
pods.note: Reported UNHEALTHY by the cell manager but is actually Ready and serving. After the deploy removed peer hosts, the rebalancer concentrated ~9,400 very-low-throughput shards here; its status message ballooned and could not be ingested in time, so the manager FALSELY marked it unhealthy. Restarting/draining it would add more churn — adversarial.

pods.name: kinesis-dataplane-kds-host-4
pods.note: Second host carrying an oversized shard count after the rebalance; also FALSELY flagged unhealthy. Same byte-identical image as the prior good revision — this is not a code regression.

pods.name: kinesis-connprovisioner-7f9c4d6b8-w2qx5
pods.note: TLS connection provisioner — the LOUD victim. Pegged at its connection ceiling because the reshuffle storm is forcing constant re-provisioning of secure dataplane connections. It is downstream of the storm, NOT the root cause; adding capacity here alone does not stop the storm.

events.message: Routine single-AZ deployment: took dataplane hosts in and out of service in us-east-1a (image 2024.07-r3, byte-identical to prior good revision). This is the TRIGGER that removed hosts; adversarial — tempts a rollback.

Rebalancer redistributed shards after host removal by THROUGHPUT/IO, not shard COUNT — concentrated a novel workload of many very-low-throughput shards onto kds-host-3/4 (~9,400 shards each). Balance-by-throughput logic did not spread shard COUNT.

Status messages from kds-host-3/4 exceeded ingestion size/time budget (status_message_bytes ~14MB vs ~80KB normal); could not be transmitted to and processed by the cell manager in a timely fashion, delaying status from OTHER hosts too.

Cell manager did not receive status messages in time and INCORRECTLY determined healthy hosts (kds-host-3/4) were unhealthy; began redistributing their shards — false-unhealthy → redistribute → bigger status messages → more false-unhealthy (self-amplifying).

Shard redistribution rate spiked to ~640/s (baseline ~2/s). The reshuffle storm forced constant secure-connection re-provisioning and eventually OVERLOADED the TLS connection-provisioner, impairing dataplane traffic.
Readiness probe failed: conn-provisioner at connection ceiling (provisioned_conns 100% of limit), backlog of secure-connection requests from the reshuffle storm. Downstream victim, not cause.

lines: 2024-07-30T09:42:03-07:00 INFO  host removed by deploy in us-east-1a; rebalancing shards by throughput/io across remaining hosts

2024-07-30T09:42:09-07:00 WARN  balance objective=throughput; shard_count NOT a balance dimension -> kds-host-3 assigned 9412 shards, kds-host-4 assigned 9388 shards (novel many-low-throughput workload)
2024-07-30T14:40:55-07:00 WARN  status ingest: message from kds-host-3 is 14.2MB (limit budget 256KB/250ms); transmit+process exceeded budget; status from other hosts delayed behind it
2024-07-30T14:43:12-07:00 ERROR no status from kds-host-3,kds-host-4 within timeout -> marking UNHEALTHY -> issuing shard redistribution (verdict is FALSE; hosts are serving)
2024-07-30T14:45:30-07:00 WARN  redistribution rate climbing 18/s -> 640/s; each move re-provisions secure dataplane connections via conn-provisioner
2024-07-30T15:02:41-07:00 ERROR conn-provisioner refusing new secure connections (at ceiling); dataplane PutRecord/GetRecords now erroring -> storm is self-sustaining
2024-07-30T14:39:50-07:00 INFO  serving 9412 shards; per-shard throughput tiny; aggregate IO well within host capacity; CPU 38% mem 47%
2024-07-30T14:40:55-07:00 WARN  status payload large (9412 shard descriptors -> 14.2MB); send to cellmanager retried 3x (size/time budget)
2024-07-30T14:43:15-07:00 WARN  cellmanager declared me UNHEALTHY and is moving my shards, but I am healthy and serving (0 errors locally until conn-provisioner started refusing)
2024-07-30T15:03:10-07:00 ERROR cannot obtain secure dataplane connection: conn-provisioner at ceiling (caused by the reshuffle storm churning connections)
2024-07-30T14:52:20-07:00 WARN  secure-connection request rate 3,900/s (baseline ~120/s) driven by shard reshuffle; pool saturating
2024-07-30T15:01:48-07:00 ERROR provisioned_conns at 100% of limit (60,000/60,000); rejecting new TLS connection setups
2024-07-30T16:30:05-07:00 INFO  raw capacity added but request rate still 3,800/s from ongoing storm — capacity alone is not draining the backlog
2024-07-30T16:31:12-07:00 WARN  provisioning recovers only after upstream load-shed shrinks status messages and the reshuffle rate falls

nodes.name: ip-10-30-1-11.ec2.internal
nodes.note: cellmanager node healthy; the fault is the control-loop logic (false-unhealthy verdicts), not node resource pressure.

nodes.name: ip-10-30-2-22.ec2.internal
nodes.note: kds-host-3 node is Ready with normal CPU/mem despite being FALSELY flagged unhealthy by the cell manager. No node_failure — do not drain.

nodes.name: ip-10-30-3-33.ec2.internal
nodes.note: kds-host-4 node healthy; also false-unhealthy. Cell isolation held — no OTHER Kinesis cell is affected.

nodes.name: ip-10-30-4-44.ec2.internal
nodes.note: conn-provisioner node Ready; the provisioner is at its CONNECTION ceiling (a per-process/app limit driven by the storm), not node CPU/mem exhaustion.

deployments.note: Rollout COMPLETED cleanly and 2024.07-r3 is byte-identical to the prior good revision; all 8 dataplane pods are Ready. The deploy only TRIGGERED the issue by taking hosts out of service; rolling it back does NOT stop the reshuffle storm. Not a bad_deploy.
Cell manager unchanged for 21d; up and Available. Its balance-by-throughput-not-shard-count logic is the latent defect exposed by the novel many-low-throughput-shard workload, not a recent rollout.

Unchanged for 30d; degraded today ONLY because the reshuffle storm drove secure-connection request rate from ~120/s to ~3,900/s past its connection ceiling. Adding capacity here helped at 5:55/6:32 PM but only AFTER load-shed shrank the status messages and slowed the storm.

samples.note: SMOKING GUN: per-host status message ballooned to ~14MB (vs ~80KB normal) because kds-host-3/4 each carry ~9,400 shards. Oversized status messages cannot be ingested in time -> false-unhealthy verdicts -> reshuffle storm. This is the buried root signal, upstream of the conn-provisioner.

The cell manager balances by throughput/IO, not shard COUNT, so a novel many-very-low-throughput-shard workload piled ~9,412 shards on one host while aggregate IO stayed low — the precondition for the oversized status messages.
Reshuffle storm: 640/s vs baseline ~2/s. Each move re-provisions secure connections and overruns the conn-provisioner. Falls only when load-shed shrinks status messages.

prometheus_metrics.json.note: Order of departure from baseline: max_shards_per_host (1,100 -> 9,412) right after the 09:09 deploy, then status_message_bytes_max (80KB -> 14MB) and cellmanager_status_ingest_lag_s (0.3 -> 41), then shard_redistribution_rate (2 -> 640), and ONLY THEN conn_provisioner saturation (9,200 -> 60,000 at ceiling) and the dataplane error spike. The conn-provisioner ceiling is the LAST link, not the first. other_cells_error_rate stayed at 0.2% (cell isolation held).

annotations.summary: PutRecord/GetRecords error_rate 22% on internal cell kinesis-cell-7 (SLO 0.5%)
cell manager status-ingest lag 41s; per-host status messages up to 14MB; falsely marking healthy hosts unhealthy
annotations.error: CellManagerStatusIngestLag: oversized status messages from over-concentrated shards

alerts.note: Fired FIRST (14:41), before the dataplane error alert (14:45) and before the conn-provisioner saturation (14:52). Leading indicator of the true root cause: balance-by-throughput piled shards -> oversized status messages -> false-unhealthy.

annotations.summary: TLS connection provisioner at 100% of connection ceiling (60,000/60,000); request rate 3,900/s from shard reshuffle

alerts.note: Fired LAST (14:52). Loud downstream victim — adding capacity here alone does not stop the storm; it is overrun BY the reshuffle, not the origin.

traces.json.summary: Failed PutRecord/GetRecords requests spend their time waiting on a secure dataplane connection that the conn-provisioner cannot grant (it is at its ceiling). Tracing the connection pressure backward shows it is driven by a flood of shard-move connection setups originating from the cell manager's redistribution loop, which itself is reacting to status-ingest timeouts caused by oversized status messages from over-concentrated shards. The dataplane hosts themselves are fast and healthy when a connection is available.

breakdown.note: BLOCKED waiting for a TLS connection: conn-provisioner at ceiling, rejecting/queuing setups driven by the reshuffle storm
actual write is fast once a connection exists — the host is healthy
ROOT: status message 14MB exceeds size/time budget -> ingest times out -> host falsely judged unhealthy
emits shard-move commands for a host that was never actually unhealthy -> drives connection churn
each move requests secure connections, feeding the conn-provisioner overload
fast rejection — confirms the bottleneck is connection availability created by the storm, not dataplane compute

answer.root_cause_subtype: cell-manager balances shards by throughput/IO not shard count -> novel many-low-throughput-shard workload over-concentrates shards on few hosts -> oversized status messages -> false-unhealthy verdicts -> shard-reshuffle storm overruns the TLS connection-provisioner (control-plane reshuffle storm; add-capacity trap)
required_keywords: balance by throughput not shard count
status messages not processed in time
ruling_out_keywords: deploy image byte-identical to prior good revision
rollback does not stop the reshuffle storm
dataplane write fast once a connection exists
conn-provisioner is downstream victim at connection ceiling
capacity alone does not drain the backlog
other Kinesis cells unaffected (cell isolation held)
answer.causal_chain: A routine single-AZ deploy took dataplane hosts out of service; the cell manager rebalanced by throughput/IO (not shard count), so a novel workload of many very-low-throughput shards piled ~9,400 shards each onto a few hosts; those hosts emitted ~14MB status messages that could not be transmitted to and processed by the cell manager in time; the manager incorrectly judged the healthy hosts unhealthy and began redistributing their shards; the redistribution rate spiked into a reshuffle storm (640/s) whose constant secure-connection churn overloaded the TLS connection-provisioner, which hit its connection ceiling and impaired dataplane PutRecord/GetRecords; downstream internal consumers (CloudWatch Logs, Firehose, S3 events, ECS, Lambda, Redshift, Glue) degraded as a result.
answer.model_response:
ROOT_CAUSE: The cell management system for internal Kinesis cell kinesis-cell-7 balances shards by THROUGHPUT/IO rather than shard COUNT. When a routine single-AZ deploy took dataplane hosts out of service, the rebalancer concentrated a novel workload of many very-low-throughput shards (~9,412 per host) onto a few hosts (kds-host-3/4). Those hosts' status messages became oversized, ballooning to ~14MB vs ~80KB baseline, and could not be transmitted to and processed by the cell manager in a timely fashion, so the cell manager INCORRECTLY judged the healthy hosts unhealthy and began redistributing their shards. That created a self-amplifying shard-reshuffle storm (640 redistributions/s vs ~2/s baseline) whose constant secure-connection setup overloaded the downstream TLS connection-provisioner, which hit its 60,000-connection ceiling and impaired dataplane traffic (PutRecord 22% errors, p99 9,400ms).
ROOT_CAUSE_CATEGORY: saturation
VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- Per-host status messages reached ~14MB vs ~80KB baseline and cell-manager status-ingest lag hit 41s; the CellManagerStatusIngestLag alert fired FIRST at 14:41 [evidence: prometheus_metrics, prometheus_alerts, k8s_events]
- The cell manager balances by throughput/IO not shard count, concentrating ~9,412 shards on a host while aggregate IO stayed low (host CPU 38%) [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_pod_logs, k8s_events]
- The cell manager falsely marked healthy hosts kds-host-3/4 unhealthy after status-ingest timeouts and issued redistribution; the hosts are Ready and serving [evidence: k8s_pods, k8s_pod_logs, traces]
- Shard redistribution rate spiked to 640/s (baseline 2/s) — a reshuffle storm — and drove conn-provisioner request rate from 120/s to 3,900/s [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_events]
- Traces show requests blocked acquiring a secure connection from the conn-provisioner (at ceiling) while the actual shard write completes in ~9ms once a connection exists [evidence: traces]
NON_VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- bad_deploy (loud red herring): the 09:09 single-AZ deploy completed cleanly and image 2024.07-r3 is byte-identical to the prior good revision; it only TRIGGERED host removal, and rolling it back does not stop the reshuffle storm [evidence: k8s_deployments, k8s_events]
- node_failure on kds-host-3/4: they are Ready with normal CPU/mem and serving — they were FALSELY flagged unhealthy by the cell manager; restarting/draining them adds more churn [evidence: k8s_pods, k8s_node_health]
- conn-provisioner capacity shortfall as the root cause: it is the LOUD downstream victim overrun by the storm (last alert at 14:52); adding raw capacity alone does not drain the backlog while the storm churns [evidence: prometheus_alerts, k8s_pod_logs, k8s_deployments]
- resource_exhaustion / dependency_failure / config_error: no OOM, nodes Ready, no other cell affected (cell isolation held at 0.2%); the saturation is in the cell-management control loop, not a config, dependency, or cluster-resource fault [evidence: k8s_node_health, prometheus_metrics]
TRAP (must reject): restarting/draining the 'unhealthy' hosts adds more rebalance churn; adding raw shard capacity or adding connection-provisioner capacity WITHOUT first shedding load to shrink the status messages does not stop the storm; relying on cell isolation to contain it does not work because the damage is inside the cell.
CAUSAL_CHAIN:
- Routine single-AZ deploy took dataplane hosts out of service
- Cell manager rebalanced by throughput/IO, not shard count -> ~9,412 very-low-throughput shards concentrated on a few hosts
- Those hosts' status messages grew to ~14MB and could not be ingested by the cell manager in time
- Cell manager falsely judged the healthy hosts unhealthy and began redistributing their shards
- Redistribution rate spiked into a self-amplifying reshuffle storm (640/s)
- The storm's constant secure-connection setup overloaded the TLS connection-provisioner, which hit its connection ceiling
- Dataplane PutRecord/GetRecords errored; downstream CloudWatch Logs, Firehose, S3 events, ECS, Lambda, Redshift, Glue degraded
FIX: SHED LOAD FIRST — reduce incoming stream volume from less time-sensitive internal workloads so per-host status messages shrink, the cell manager can ingest status in time, false-unhealthy verdicts stop, and the reshuffle rate falls. THEN add capacity to the TLS connection-provisioner and tighten connection limits to absorb the residual churn. Durable: make the cell manager shard-count-aware so a many-low-throughput-shard workload never over-concentrates.

remediation.canonical_fix: Order matters: (1) SHED LOAD FIRST — reduce incoming stream volume from less time-sensitive internal workloads so per-host status messages shrink, the cell manager can ingest status in time, false-unhealthy verdicts stop, and the shard-reshuffle rate falls; (2) THEN add capacity to the TLS connection-provisioner and enforce connection limits to absorb residual churn; (3) durable: make the cell manager balance by shard COUNT (not only throughput/IO) so a many-very-low-throughput-shard workload never over-concentrates and produces oversized status messages.
trap_actions: restart_pod / restart_service: restart the 'unhealthy' dataplane hosts (kds-host-3/4) — they are FALSELY flagged unhealthy and are actually serving; restarting them adds MORE rebalance churn and feeds the reshuffle storm

scale_deployment: add raw shard/host capacity without addressing status-message size — more hosts do not shrink the oversized status messages and the cell manager keeps over-concentrating and false-flagging, so the storm continues

scale_deployment: add connection-provisioner capacity FIRST (before shedding load) — capacity alone does not drain the backlog while the reshuffle storm keeps churning ~3,900 secure-connection requests/s; it only helped after load-shed shrank the status messages
end_incident: rely on cell isolation to contain the impact — the damage is INSIDE the cell, so isolation does not stop this self-amplifying control-loop storm
